F#%K it. I'm gonna talk about it.
This isn't a happy post. But it's real.

When you start a business, you think the people close to you will support you.

You think your family will cheer for you.
You think your friends will be proud of you.

But many times, they don't say anything.

They watch what you do and see your posts.
They don't send a single message.
They stay quiet.

And then something strange happens.
People you don't even know say:
"You're doing great. Keep going."

A simple message from a stranger feels warmer than silence from someone you love.

Running a business is already hard.

You get tired and scared.
You wonder if you made the right choice.

You work when everyone else is sleeping.
You give everything for something you believe in.

So when the people you love stay quiet, it hurts.
You don't want praise.

You just hoped they cared enough to notice how hard you're trying.

But strangers see your effort today.
They see your courage.
They see your consistency.

And they appreciate it.
To the people who support us, thank you.
Your support keeps us going on the tough days.

To the quiet ones, thank you too.
Your silence shows us who is really with us.

If you're building something, choose your circle wisely.
Stay close to people who want you to grow.

P.S. Has a stranger ever supported you more than someone close to you?

content:
Every founder asks the same question on Friday.
"Wait… what did we actually get done this week?"

I used to dig through Slack, emails, and random docs just to piece together a weekly update.

The work was getting done. 
And it just stayed invisible.

I fixed this in 5 minutes using Claude Cowork.

Here's how:

Create a folder on your computer. Name it "Weekly Updates."

All week, drag files into it. Notes. Docs and anything you worked on.

Open Claude Cowork. Click "Work in a Folder." Select that folder.

Connect Gmail, Drive, and Slack under Settings → Connectors.

Go to Settings → Cowork → Global Instructions. 
Tell it exactly how you want your weekly report formatted.

Type /schedule and set it for every Friday at 4PM.
That's it.

So every Friday at 4 PM, Cowork reads your folder, pulls from Gmail, Drive, and Slack, and builds one clean report.

You write zero words.
By the end of the quarter, you have a full record of everything your business actually built.

P.S.How do you track what your team gets done every week?

document.title: The Old vs New Way of Using Claude for Your LinkedIn

content:
If your LinkedIn posts sound robotic, this 10-minute fix changes everything.

Here's the system:
Step 1: Record yourself for 5 minutes
Talk about your work naturally and transcribe with Otter.ai.

Step 2: Extract your Voice DNA
Paste transcript into Claude:
"Extract my unique phrases, sentence patterns, and tone.
Identify 10 words I use repeatedly.

Step 3: Ban AI phrases.

List words you never use: "delve, unlock, leverage, synergy, here's the thing."
Upload to Claude. Tell it to avoid these.

Step 4: Kill generic sentences

"Remove any sentence that works for both LinkedIn marketing AND pasta recipes.
Make everything specific."

Step 5: Add imperfections
"Add line breaks every 2-3 sentences.
Start some with 'And' or 'But'.

Step 6: Test before posting

Use GPTZero.me. If flagged, run this:
"This reads as AI. Make it conversational.
Remove robotic patterns. Add personality."

Retest until it passes.
It takes 10 minutes per post and sounds exactly like you.

content:
I had to look my team in the eye and tell them it was over.


That was the hardest day of my life.

But just a few years before that, I was on television, in newspapers, everywhere.
I thought I had made it.

Then everything collapsed.

And I mean everything:
My personal life, my professional goals, my team. 

People I cared about lost their jobs because of me.

I felt like a failure.

I had disappointed my family, my employees, and everyone around me.

I even asked myself:
What's the point of all of this?

That's the lowest I've ever felt.

But that period changed me in ways the good times never could.

Life doesn't just bring you to your knees to hurt you.
It brings you there to rebuild you.

I started taking responsibility and stopped putting all my eggs in one basket.  

I became the hardest-working person in every room I walked into.

And then I changed who I was in the room with.

If there were 10 people smarter than me, I figured out that was the right room.

If there were 15 people richer than me, I figured out that was the right room.

And I stopped spending time with people who didn't pull me up.

Your worst moment is not your final moment.
It's the moment that decides who you actually become.

P.S. What do you do when you're on your knees?

content:
I smoked a pack a day for almost two years.

And no one knew except my sister about this.

I never touched a cigarette in my entire adult life.

But I went through a rough patch. 

And when you completely break, you do things you've never done before.

I didn't do it once or twice.
I did it every single day, for almost two years. 

Because that's how different I was as a person.

Then my daughter was born.
And something shifted.

You know how some people carry the smell of smoke with them everywhere they go? 
I grew up around that. 

I knew exactly what that association felt like.

And I never wanted my daughter to grow up associating that smell with her dad.
The day she was born, I stopped. Just like that.

No one told me to, and nothing changed externally.
But I finally had something more important than the pain.

So here's what I've seen with founders.
Most of them aren't stuck because they lack discipline or ideas.

They struggle because they don't have a clear enough reason to show up.

The moment you get real clarity on who they serve, what they stand for, and what they're building, everything changes.

And this happened because it finally means something.

P.S. Has there been a moment that made you decide to show up differently?

content:
Last week one of my posts went viral.
(But it brought zero leads to me)

That’s the founder visibility trap.

More reach but less qualified leads.

Here’s what most founder don’t see.

LinkedIn’s feed now behaves in two buckets:

Universal content:
→ Broad, viral, easy to agree with.

Niche content:
→ Specific, targeted, authority-driven.

Universal spreads fast.
But it spreads to everyone:

Students. Job seekers. Other creators.
People who like your post but will never buy.

Niche spreads slower.
But it spreads to the right people.

Buyers with the exact problem you solve.

That is why a 100,000-impression post can generate fewer qualified leads
than a 2,000-impression post.

Because virality sends you wide.
But relevance sends you deep.

And here’s the expensive myth:

Visibility does not equal revenue.

Undifferentiated visibility trains the algorithm
to show your content to the wrong audience.

Your Relevance Score drops.
Your pipeline dries up.

Look at how top creators on LinkedIn are writing now:

- More specific.
- More personal.
- More narrow.

They’re trading viral potential
for depth of connection.

P.S. Would you rather have 10,000 people see your post
Or 500 of your exact buyers?

You have to mine problems, not the random posts.

content:
If you’re not an immigrant, don’t watch this.
You won’t understand it anyway.

People don’t leave their country because they’re brave.
They leave because they have no choice.

Work.
Money.
Dreams.
Survival.
Sometimes all at once.

At first, it feels exciting.
New city. New faces. New rules.
You say, “I’ll stay a few years.”

Years pass fast.

One day you realize something that’s hard to explain.
You don’t fully belong where you are.
And you don’t fully belong back home either.

You live in between.

Loneliness isn’t loud.
It’s quiet.

It’s laughing at jokes you don’t fully get.
Festivals that feel empty.
Conversations where you smile, but your mind is somewhere else.

You call home.
Mom asks, “Did you eat?”
Dad says, “Everything is fine.”

But you hear it.
Their voices are slower now.
They’re getting older.

You miss birthdays.
Weddings.
Normal days.

Relatives turn into calls.
Calls turn into messages.
Messages turn into memories.

And you keep going.
Because what’s the alternative?

Then comes that one flight back home.

Same airport.
Same smell.
Same noise.

You see them.
Older. Much older.
Smiling like you never left.

First question they ask isn’t about money or success.
It’s “What do you want to eat?”

They buy things you loved as a kid.
Things you forgot existed.

Relatives drop everything and show up.
The house is the same.
The bed.
The fan noise at night.
The voices calling your name.

For a few days, you belong.
Fully.

You sleep better.
You laugh more.
You feel lighter.

You’re not “the immigrant.”
You’re not “the one who left.”

You’re just you.

Then you leave again.

Same goodbye.
Mom looks away.
Dad stays strong.

“Call when you land.”

You always promise you’ll come back soon.

You land.
Different airport.

No one waiting.
No one asking what you want to eat.
No familiar voices.

You drop your bag.
Sit down.

And it hits you.

Not because this place is bad.
But because love didn’t follow you back.

Living abroad means you win and you lose.

You gain opportunity.
Freedom.
Money.

You lose being understood without explaining.
You lose warmth.
You lose home as a feeling.

You start belonging everywhere.
And nowhere.

If this feels familiar, you’re not weak.
You’re not ungrateful.

You’re human.

This isn’t just my story.
It’s millions of people living between two homes.

Holding both.
Knowing they never fully will.

That’s the part no one talks about.

AI helps you move faster.

and forget to solve the present.

Money doesn’t come from sounding smart.

Sometimes the fastest path forward is boring, simple, and obvious.

And when that happens, business stops feeling like hope.
It starts feeling like cash flow.

Solve something real.
Even if it’s boring.

That’s how businesses actually get built.

content:
You’re probably asking the wrong question about AI.

Everyone keeps asking, What can it do now?

What can’t it do?

Because once AI makes things easy and everywhere, value moves.
It stops living in speed, volume, or output.

It hides in what’s hard. And scarce.

Here’s what happens then.

AI can give you data in seconds.

It can spot patterns and follow instructions perfectly.
It can even sound like it had a breakthrough in the shower.

But when the data is messy?
When the answer isn’t clear?
When there’s real risk attached?

That’s where it stops.

AI can’t make the tough call.
It can’t sit with uncertainty and still choose.

It can’t carry the weight of being wrong.

And that’s the thing most people miss.
People don’t pay you for skills anymore.
They pay for judgment.

Your own taste, experience, and ability to make good decisions when there’s no clean answer.

That’s also how trust gets built, not through perfect logic.
Through consistently sound calls over time.

That’s the moat, AI will never cross it.

So stop trying to compete on speed or output.
That race never ends well.

Start cultivating the one thing it can’t touch.
Your human judgment.

And one more thing.
Don’t let that edge stay hidden.

Don’t be the best-kept secret.

document.title: Your Brand Has No Authority on LinkedIn

content:
That cheap AI tool you pay monthly is not actually cheap.
It feels affordable because the subscription price looks harmless today.

But that price hides real infrastructure and computing costs.
Most AI tools operate under heavy VC subsidy models.

They burn capital to cover GPU usage and training expenses.
This is the VC subsidy trap that few founders model properly.

Your favorite AI platform stays cheap only while funding continues.
Some providers lose money on every active paid subscriber.

The real unit economics are far higher than public pricing.

When capital tightens, pricing models change without warning.
Costs spike, features lock, or platforms shut down entirely.
That is not a theory, it is structural risk.

Founders build workflows assuming tool stability and price certainty.
But the foundation depends on external investor patience.

That is a fragile dependency for serious businesses.

Buying more SaaS tools does not reduce platform risk.
It increases operational and vendor exposure instead.

The real hedge is skill, judgment, and system design.
Decision frameworks survive pricing shifts and vendor exits.

Strategic thinking cannot be deprecated or rate-limited.

AI should augment cognition, not replace decision ownership.
And Automation without understanding creates silent failure points.

So, founders who outsource thinking lose leverage when markets reset.

Use AI tools, but design for volatility and churn.

Build systems you can adapt, not subscriptions you depend on.
Because when the market turns, systems survive, tools disappear.

content:
I shouldn’t have started a business.

Being an employee would’ve been easier.
Clear hours. Clear rules. Less doubt at 2 a.m.

I still remember one moment that made my stomach drop.

We published a cheat sheet on a client’s profile.
A tortoise inside it.... a damn tortoise!!
Completely wrong.

I stared at the screen thinking, This is what I left safety for?

Then came the harder moments.

When you lead with kindness and a client treats it like weakness.
When you realise being good at your work doesn’t protect you from being tested.
That’s when quitting stops being a thought and starts feeling reasonable.

There were days I opened my laptop and wished someone would tell me to stop trying.
Not motivate me.
Just give me permission to walk away without shame.

Most days didn’t feel like progress.
They felt like holding it together.

I kept talking to people anyway.
Most conversations went nowhere.
A few showed me what worked and what didn’t.

My first offer wasn’t good.
My first client didn’t get my best work.
And that wasn’t failure. That was the work.

If you’re early in business and thinking,
“I should’ve just stayed employed,”
you’re not broken.

You’re early.
You’re exposed.
And that part is uncomfortable for everyone.

Take the step anyway.
Don’t do it alone.
And don’t confuse discomfort with the wrong path.

That feeling is the price of becoming someone new.

document.title: Stop Using ChatGPT for Everything

content:
F#%K it. I’m gonna talk about it.
This isn’t a happy post, but it’s real.

When you start a business, you think the people close to you will support you.
You think your family will cheer for you.
You think your friends will be proud of you.

But many times, they don’t say anything.
They watch what you do.
They see your posts.
They don’t press like.
They don’t send a message.
They stay quiet.

And then something strange happens.
People you don’t even know say,
“You’re doing great. Keep going.”
A simple message from a stranger feels warmer than silence from someone you love.

Running a business is already hard.
You get tired.
You get scared.
You wonder if you made the right choice.
You work when everyone else is sleeping.
You give everything for something you believe in.

So when the people you love stay quiet, it hurts.
Not because you want praise.
But because you hoped they cared enough to notice how hard you’re trying.

Strangers can support you more easily because they don’t compare you to who you used to be.
They see your effort today.
They see your courage.
They see your consistency.
And they appreciate it.

To the people who support us, thank you.
You mean more than you know.
Your words help us keep going on the tough days.

To the quiet people in our lives, thank you too.
Your silence shows us who is really with us.

If you’re building something, choose your circle wisely.
Stay close to people who want you to grow.
Create a group where everyone helps each other.

And if you ever need someone to stand with you, reach out.
I’ll support you. I know how this journey feels.

content:
Your content could train the next AI model.
But only if you do this first..

Start by giving clear answers, right at the top.

AI pulls from your intro first. 
If you hide your point, you disappear.

Write like this: Question to answer, and then details.

AI engines love clarity. 
They scan for questions, 
Then surface the cleanest response.

Keep your best posts fresh.
Update your top five every 90 days, add new data, insights, or stories.

Back up what you say.
Add links, research, and real examples inside your text.
AI trusts what’s cited, not what just sounds smart.

Give structure.
Use the FAQ or How-To schema on your site.
That’s the roadmap AI uses to read your content clearly.

Build topic clusters.
Pick one hub like AI for Founders and link 5-10 posts to it.
That’s how you become the go-to voice in your niche.

Show up in communities.

Reddit threads, Slack groups, 
Niche forums, one thoughtful answer there can 
Get quoted thousands of times by AI models.

Publish your own data.
Surveys, benchmarks, and studies make you the source 
And AI cites the source first.

If you are still wondering if SEO matters? It does.

AI crawls what Google does.
Strong rankings and backlinks still feed AI visibility.

And finally, track your own citations.
Search your brand in ChatGPT or Perplexity.
See what’s working, refresh it, repeat.

AI visibility isn’t luck. 
Its structure, clarity, and proof were repeated.

content:
“They call me stupid.”
Maybe they’re right. But not for the reasons they think.

They said it made no sense.
Finland → Manila → Finland → Kuala Lumpur… all within days.
More than 24 hours one way.
Ten-hour delays.
Waiting at the airport
Two-hour trains.
Walk back home in the cold.

“Why would you do that?
Just for two days?
It’s stupid.”

Maybe to them it is.

But when your daughter is waiting at the door, smiling like you’re the whole world… 
Logic becomes tiny. And love becomes loud.

I spent those 48 hours doing nothing extraordinary.
Just toys.
Just hugs.
Just presence.
And those moments rearranged my entire definition of “worth it.”

People judge based on what they value.
Parents act based on what matters.

content:
I stopped writing my own posts.
But my voice got stronger using AI, here is why:

Most people tell Claude, “Write me a post.”

Then they get something that sounds smart, 
But not like them.

If you want Claude or any LLM to write in your voice, 
You need to train it like a team member, not a tool.

Start with context.
Tell it who you are, who you serve, and what you’re building.
That’s your role and goal, the GPS needs to stay accurate.

Then set the tone.
Don’t say “professional.” Say “warm, sharp, and strategic.”

Be clear about how you want to sound, 
Not what you want to say.

Next, feed it your world.
Upload your best posts, brand docs, and ICPs
Everything that shows your logic and rhythm.

Claude learns from examples, not adjectives.

Give it rules.
Start with a bold hook. Short paragraphs. 
End with a question.

Rules stop drift. 
Without them, AI gets creative in all the wrong ways.

And finally, iterate.
Say, “Refine the hook from your last draft.”

Don’t restart every time. Train it through repetition.

You’re not teaching AI to write.
You’re teaching it to think like you.

I was invited by Success Resources to speak in front of 800 people.

But selling to a large audience is tough, 
especially for the first time.

It takes years of practice, trial, and failure to master it.

On stage, I felt prepared but still panicked 
when something unexpected happened.

One person said another speaker would come help, 
but didn’t explain why.

My mind started wandering, focusing on everything but the sale.

In the end, I made 97 sales, not the 100 I aimed for.

I was devastated, but I took full responsibility for the failure.

Fast forward: the cohort began. I gave my all.
My team gave 100%. And the people were moved.

They were truly happy and became the biggest advocates.
Some cried. Others were emotional, sharing how much value they received.

It wasn’t just the material they learned, it was the change they felt.

The transformation wasn’t just in the knowledge, but in their hearts.

Afterward, many stayed back, and some even became friends.

That’s when it hit me.

Numbers matter, but impact matters more.
I stopped chasing numbers. I focused on creating impact.

And over time, the numbers grew and compounded.

If you're chasing numbers too, remember this:
Focus on the impact, and the numbers will follow.

content:
You think you know how AI reads prompts.
But it's not magic, it's math.

When you type a request, the model doesn’t read like us.

It breaks your words into tiny chunks called tokens 
LinkedIn is one, founder-led business is three.

Then it predicts, one token at a time, 
What you probably want next.

Behind the scenes, there are hidden rules 
System instructions that decide tone, safety, and structure.

You can’t see them, but they shape every response.

The model remembers your recent chats 
Through something called a context window.

That’s why earlier messages 
Still influence the next one, until the window fills up.

And no, it doesn’t learn from you in real time.
Your feedback helps future versions, not this one.

So when your prompt feels off, 
It’s not because the AI ignored you 
It’s because your input wasn’t clear enough on how it actually thinks.

Write like you’re giving directions, not ideas.
Be specific. Use examples. Define your tone.

AI isn’t guessing your mind 
It’s following your math.

You open your LinkedIn inside Comet,

- About section with one strong proof point and a call-to-action.

And the difference is:
More people stopped scrolling.
More people messaged.

And the right people actually booked calls.

Your LinkedIn isn’t failing because you lack skill.
It’s failing because it can’t pass the 3-second test.

Fix that, and everything changes.

document.title: Stuck on LinkedIn Perplexity can help fix it

content:
Your friend with a 9-to-5 just got paid.
He worked 40 hours. He made $2,000.

You worked 80 hours. You made $500.

Welcome to the founder life, the only job
where working harder can still pay less.

It’s the rollercoaster we all ride.

One week you’re celebrating new clients,
the next you’re chasing invoices and fixing tech issues at 2 a.m.

But that $500 isn’t failure.
It’s a seed. 

A small proof you’re building something real.

You’re not trading time for money anymore,
You’re turning ideas into assets.

Every post, every client call, every late night
is stacking invisible equity, in your brand, your reach, your system.

You trade short-term comfort for long-term control.
You build something no boss, algorithm, or market dip can take away.

Freedom.

So yeah, you made less this week.

But you’re buying back your future, one hard day at a time.
And that’s a payoff no 9-to-5 will ever match.

content:
Most founders search for trends the wrong way.
They open Google, type what’s trending. 

And hope for gold.

But by the time Google shows it, it’s already old news.
Because Google only shows what’s already been indexed.

You’re not early, you’re six months late.

I learned that the hard way.
Until I started looking where trends are born, not published.

That place: Reddit.

People complain before they buy.
They ask questions before solutions exist.

And those early rants? 
They’re trend signals in disguise.

So I changed my method:
I go to Reddit, type my niche, and filter for “Top” posts in the past week.

Then I read.

- Every frustration is a content idea.
- Every “I wish” is a product gap.
- Every “Does anyone know how to”

It's a hook waiting to happen.

That’s how I stay ahead.
Reddit gives me the pain points.
Claude turns it into actionable posts.

And Perplexity helps me check the facts.

Most founders post what they think matters.
The best founders post what people are already asking for.

That’s how you find trends before anyone else does.

document.title: You're searching for trends wrong.

content:
We all hide behind perfection.
But perfection is just fear wearing designer clothes.

You spent six months perfecting your product. 
Every pixel. Every feature.

Then you spent six days trying to sell it.
A few posts. One email. Then silence.

And now you wonder why no one is buying.

Here’s the truth:
No one cares how great your product is if no one sees it.

You don’t have a marketing problem.
You have a visibility problem.

You’re not just a builder.
You’re a storyteller, a marketer, a founder who happens to have a product.

Most founders stop at launch:

They think the build was the finish line.
But the real game starts when you step into the market.

Every day you don’t talk about your product, it dies quietly.

You need to spend just as much time selling as building.
Talk to people. Post your story. Share your process.

Write copy. Send DMs. Ask for feedback.

A messy product with great marketing wins every time.
A perfect one with no noise stays invisible.

Stop hiding behind your code.
Go tell people why it matters.

Then sell the thing.

content:
ChatGPT has 800 million weekly users.
But it’s not always the best way to search with AI.

I learned that the hard way.
I ran the same 9 search queries across seven different AI tools 
Just to see who wins.

Each one had its own superpower.
Each one was better at something different.

Perplexity crushed it for news and research.
It gave me live data, sources, and people's info in seconds.

ChatGPT ruled product searches, specs, 
reviews, and pricing fast and clean.

Gemini beat everyone at YouTube and legal research.

It found key moments, laws, and citations without me asking twice.
Claude nailed deep analysis, the kind that simplifies messy, complex ideas.

And Grok: It turned Reddit chaos into clear, human pain points.

That’s when it hit me.
We’ve all been using one AI like it’s the only tool.

But there’s no single winner.
There’s just the right AI for the right job.

So next time you open ChatGPT, ask yourself:
Is this the best tool for this task?
Or just the most familiar one?

Stop using one AI for everything.
Start using the right AI for anything.

Everyone cheers when you win.

But no one sees you
when it’s 2 AM and you’re still working.

No one sees the long nights,
the failed launches, the quiet room.

Your friends are out.
Your family is asleep.

You’re at your desk.

You check the numbers again.
You wonder if you made a mistake.

You think about your team,
your customers, your dream.

It’s heavy. It’s lonely. And it’s all on you.

They call it “CEO.”
But it feels more like “Chief Everything Officer.”

You fix what’s broken.
You handle the mess. You carry the load.

When things go wrong, there’s no one else to blame.
And when you fall, no one comes to lift you up.

But here’s the truth:
You’re not really alone.
Every founder feels this.

Every builder knows this silence.

It’s the price you pay for creating
something that matters.

So if you’re sitting in that
quiet room tonight, don’t stop.

Keep going. Keep building. Keep believing.

The world needs what you’re making,
even if no one sees it yet.

Because the loneliest job in the world
is also the one that changes it.

You post what feels right, hope it lands, and move on

Built conversations, not just clicks.

Download your LinkedIn data. Upload it to ChatGPT.

Ask what’s working, and why.
You’ll be surprised how fast clarity shows up.

Your story deserves to be seen.
Let data, and a little AI, help it shine.

content:
Everyone's using GPT-5 at half its power.
And they wonder why outputs feel generic still.

When I first started using GPT-5, 
I did what most founders do.

I opened it, typed a few things, and expected magic.
Instead, I got random answers that didn’t match what I needed.

That’s when I realized, GPT-5 is like a team, not a tool.
Each mode has its own way of thinking and working.

Now, when I need quick help or short ideas, 
I use Instant Mode.

It’s fast, simple, and perfect for brainstorming or rewriting.

When I need a strategy or a content plan, 
I switch to Thinking Mode.

It slows down and helps me connect ideas like a partner would.

For deep work, research, audits, long docs, 
I open Pro Mode.

It feels like having an expert who thinks for you with precision.

And when I don’t know what to choose, 
I trust Auto Mode.

It picks the right level of thinking based on my question.

The best part is GPT-5 keeps getting smarter with me.
It remembers my past chats, knows my tone, 
And saves time.

It can even browse the web, run projects, 
And build side-by-side.

Learning to work with GPT-5 changed how I work every day.
Less guessing. More clarity. More results.

If you’re still typing random prompts, stop.
Work smarter, pick the right mode, and 
Let GPT-5 do the heavy lifting.

content:
I used to work in a company where there was this one guy.
 He was the smartest person in the room.
 Everyone went to him for advice.
 He always had the right answers.
 He knew how every system worked.
 He was respected.
 He was admired.
 And I remember thinking, this guy will build something huge one day.

But years passed.
 He never did.
 He kept saying, “Not yet. I’m still thinking it through.”
 He saw every risk before it even happened.
 His brain could solve every problem - except his own fear.

Then there was another guy.
 He got fired.
 Everyone said his idea was dumb.
 He didn’t wait.
 He didn’t plan.
 He just started.

Five years later, the smart one was still in the same meetings.
 The fired one was running his own team.
 Both were smart.
 But one was comfortable.
 The other was hungry.

And here’s what I’ve learned.
 It’s not a must to start your own business.
 Some people are happy where they are, and that’s okay.
 But the quiet regret of “I could have…”
 is heavier than the failure of “I tried.”

content:
Everyone’s moving faster than you think.
Because they stopped giving their work unlimited time.

But you’re a founder at 11 p.m.
Convincing yourself it’s fine to keep working.

You’re not driven.

You’re trapped by Parkinson’s Law:
Work expands to fill the time you give it.

If you give yourself a week, it’ll take a week.
If you give yourself a day, it’ll take a day.

And you’re not doing more.
You’re just stretching the same work across more hours.

That’s the great hoax of hustle culture.

It tells you that more hours = more output.
But it’s a lie.

It keeps you busy, not better.
You feel productive, but you’re stuck on the same wheel.

Real progress needs artificial constraints.

So, give yourself tight deadlines.

- One day to write a week’s content.
- One hour to plan your next launch.

You’ll focus on the 20% that drives 80% of results.
The rest is just noise.

Don’t be the best-kept secret.
Be the one who finishes fast and grows faster.

You underestimate the power of a good ChatGPT prompt.
I did too.

Back then, I was a founder with smart ideas
But no one saw them.

I stared at the screen, typed vague asks,
And got replies that sounded flat and robotic.

The real problem wasn’t the tool.
It was how I was using it.

I was tired, burned out, invisible online
And my prompts only made it worse.

Then I started building rules.
Simple rules that turned random replies
Into content that actually sounded like me.

Suddenly, I wasn’t wasting hours rewriting. I was saving them.

Here are the 9 rules I live by:

1. Give clear context
2. Be specific
3. Skip vague asks
4. Break into steps
5. Ask for examples
6. Choose the format
7. Assign a role
8. Treat it like a human
9. Refine and retry

These rules don’t make AI write for you.
They make AI speak like you on your best day, every day.

document.title: 9 Prompts Rules That Unlock ChatGPT 5 for you

content:
I stood before 800 people, and halfway through, I realized I was failing in real time.

Rotterdam.
The lights were blinding.
My palms were sweaty.
My voice sounded smaller than usual.
I had practised for weeks, maybe months.
My goal was simple: to sell hundreds of students into my next cohort.

 But somewhere in the middle, I felt it.
 The room got quiet.
 Not the kind of quiet that means focus the kind that makes you question yourself.
 My words started to feel heavy.
 The connection wasn’t there.

That’s when I knew I had set the wrong goal.
 I was chasing numbers, not meaning.
 Sales, not impact.

And yet, when the course ended, something unexpected happened.

 People were crying.
 They said it was one of the best courses they’d ever taken.
 They said I was patient.
 They said the community felt like family.
 That helping each other was the best part of it all.

 That moment changed everything.
 I realized I had been looking at success the wrong way.
 Yes, I still want to become the best salesperson on the planet.

 But real mastery takes time.
 It takes patience.
 It takes practice.

 Rotterdam taught me that failure is not the opposite of success.
 It’s part of it.
 Failing wasn’t the end.
 It was the beginning of getting better.

Big thanks to Success Resources June Yoon

content:
Most founders post daily and book nothing.
But the top 1% follow these three levels instead.

Here's the path they take and 
Why skipping any step keeps you stuck.

Level 1: Brand Foundation

This is where most people rush past.
And that is the biggest mistake.

You need to know who you're talking to, 
What makes you different, and how you sound.

Define your ICP. 
Lock in your positioning. 

Build your messaging. Tune your tone of voice.

It takes 2–4 days, but it's the difference between random 
posts and a system that converts.

Without this, everything you build later falls apart.

Level 2: Strategic Content

Now you actually start posting.
But not random thoughts with proven formats. 
Consistent rhythm and testing what lands.

Your profile views start to increase. Engagement picks up.
First few DMs trickle in. Maybe calls and conversion.
This takes 30–60 days of showing up with intention.

And most founders think this is the final level, but it's not.

Level 3: Authority System

This is where deals flow on repeat.
Your content is voice-calibrated. Your hooks solve real pain. 

Your CTAs move people to action.
And you're tracking the pipeline. Optimizing daily. Refining what works.

The results are: 
3–6 booked calls every week. Faster close rates. Predictable revenue.
Getting here takes 6–12 months, but that's how you build a 7-figure 
pipeline from LinkedIn alone.

If you skip a level, you will stay invisible.

content:
I blamed AI for killing my voice.
But I was the one using it wrong.

Most founders I talk to say the same thing.
They’ve tried using AI to post on LinkedIn 
But it never sounds like them.

The words feel cold. The tone feels off.
So they stop posting and go quiet again.

I’ve been there too.
Running a business, 
You barely have time to think, let alone write.

You open ChatGPT, type a few prompts, 
And it gives you something that sounds robotic.
You stare at it thinking, “This doesn’t feel like me.”

That’s when I learned the real problem isn’t AI.
It’s the way we use it.

Most founders treat AI like a tool.
But it should be treated like a system that learns your voice.

So I built one.
I created an AI brain that remembers 
My tone, my story, and my clients.

It’s trained on everything 
About my brand, my offers, my ICP, my message.

Now, every time I create content, 
It starts from my truth, not a blank page.

When I want to find new ideas, 
I use AI to study what my audience actually feels.

Their fears, their frustrations, their hopes.
It’s like reading their minds, but with data.
That’s how I write posts that feel real and connect instantly.

Then I pass my draft to Claude.
Claude doesn’t change my voice, it warms it up.

It adds flow, rhythm, and heart.
Suddenly, what was robotic becomes relatable.

Now my content sounds human, 
Takes half the time, and actually books clients.
Because AI has amplified me.

If you’ve ever felt invisible online,
Build your system before you blame the tool.

AI won’t make you sound fake
If you teach it, who you really are.

document.title: How to create content with AI that actually sounds YOU

content:
The hardest part isn’t the tool.
It’s convincing yourself to start with one idea.

Most founders tell me the same thing:
I don’t know what to post.

That fear is real.
It’s easier to stay silent than risk posting something off.
And when you add AI into the mix, it feels even heavier.

But I tested something different. I opened Google Veo 3.
Typed one simple line: “8-second video of a lemon being sliced.”

Seconds later, there it was.
A clean, short video ready to download and share.
No cameras, no editing, and no waiting.

That’s when it clicked: AI isn’t about being perfect.
It’s about trying one small thing today.

If a sliced lemon can turn into a video, so can your next idea.
The only real block is overthinking.

content:
Your story is the algorithm that AI can't replace.
That’s why founder-led branding still wins pipelines in 2025.

I see it every week.
A founder discovers AI. Gets excited and starts writing posts.
Templates everywhere. Automation on autopilot. Content flying out daily.

And then nothing happens:
Generic voice. A few shallow likes. Zero calls booked.

They're using AI wrong.
Here's what actually works:

You don't start with the tool. 
You start with your story.
Who you are. What you've built. 

The problems you solve that no one else sees the same way.
Then you tune AI to that, your voice, your perspective, your best thinking.
So, when do you draft a post?

It sounds like you on your sharpest day. 
Not like everyone else using the same prompt.

That's the difference between AI that clutters your feed 
and AI that fills your calendar.

Bad AI strategy looks like this:

Chasing more posts with less clarity.
Sounds like a bot. 

Impressions that don't pay bills. 
The founder thinks, "AI will do this for me."

A good AI strategy looks like this:
Focused on booked calls and inbound leads. 

Sounds unmistakably like you. 

And the Metrics are:
Clients in the pipeline. 

The founders winning on LinkedIn in 2025 aren't posting faster.
They're showing up with a system that works even when 
they're buried in client delivery.

AI doesn't replace your voice.
It turns your story into a client engine that runs while you sleep.

That's the real future of founder branding.

content:
We all have that one skill we're terrified to show.
For me, it was speaking English in front of people.

Back then, I struggled with English.
I grew up speaking Tamil, not English.
In school, we never practiced English.

So when I started teaching dance, I was terrified.
I had to speak to students, but my voice shook.
My mind froze, and fear took over.

So I practiced for hours in front of the toilet door mirror, 
taking a toothbrush in hand as a mic while water ran through the tap. 

I kept talking to myself until the words finally came out.

It wasn’t pretty.
It wasn’t polished.
But it was practice.

And that’s what most founders miss.
They wait until their content feels perfect.
Until they feel ready.

So they never post at all.
But authority doesn’t come from perfection.
It comes from showing up, awkward, messy, and nervous.

Because every rep compounds.
Those shaky speeches led me to global stages.
And to people like June Yoon .

She saw something in me that I didn’t see in myself.
She believed, she coached, and she opened doors
I thought they were locked forever.

Today, she’s my friend, my teacher, and still my coach.
And I coach her, too.

We’ve grown together, as friends, leaders, and humans.
That never would’ve happened if I stayed silent.

And for this, you don’t need to be perfect to start.
You need to be consistent enough to be seen.

Show up scared.
Show up messy.
But show up.

Because doors only open when you’re visible.
June, thank you for trusting me, for teaching me,

And for proving that kindness, combined with visibility, changes everything.

By 2026, I promise this:
I will be one of the best salespeople on stage.

content:
When clients son starts calling you uncle
You know you've cracked the code.

For me, it changed how I see business forever.

Sometimes a client walks into your life,
And you expect it to stay professional.

But then trust grows, value multiplies,
And suddenly, it’s much more than a deal.

Six months ago, I started working with Mehsum.

His mission was bold: 
Solving visa struggles for people with weak passports.
He was prepared, proactive, and relentless in every single meeting.

Our first post together went viral.
Millions of impressions, thousands of likes,
And a pipeline that wouldn’t stop filling.

But the real story wasn’t the numbers.
It was what happened in Singapore.

I met his family during the National Achievers Congress.
We spoke about business, but also about life.

His son Shamir asked me,
Can you get Robert Kiyosaki’s autograph for me?

That was the moment I realized
this wasn’t just business anymore.
They started calling me Danny uncle.

A few weeks later, his kids messaged me for a call.
It wasn’t about leads. It was about connection.

Here’s what I’ve learned:
When you give 100% and create real value,
Clients become friends, and friends become allies,
And allies open doors you never imagined.

Today, Mehsum is thriving, closing big deals,
And I’m proud to call him not just a client
But a friend.

document.title: The AI stack that turned your LinkedIn to client magnet

content:
Most founders don’t fail because their offer is bad.
They fail because buyers never even see them.

And here are the hidden layers they miss 👇

1. Surface Symptoms

Let’s just post more; it feels safe, but it doesn’t work.
You post features, not stories, but your buyers keep scrolling.

Because templates or ghostwriters erase your voice, but trust dies.

You get likes, but no DMs.
You entertain strangers instead of qualifying buyers.

2. Real Strategy Gaps

You have no clear strategy and messaging.
Jargon hides the real outcomes buyers want.

You sound like every competitor who shouts louder.
You don’t have any workflow trust fades before it compounds.

3. Root Causes

Your ICPs don’t connect with faceless brands.
They buy from founders who share their story.

So, if you try to speak to everyone, no one pays a premium.
If you treat visibility like vanity, competitors steal your pipeline.

And the fix isn’t posting harder.
It is building a system where your story books clients.

Your story should work overtime.
We make sure it does.
